rubric.task_summary: Implement efficient prime check for n up to 10^7 in Python, handling edge cases correctly.
failure_modes: Time limit exceeded for large primes

assumptions: Input n is always an integer.
The upper bound of 10,000,000 is inclusive.
known_risks: For n very close to 10^7, the loop runs up to sqrt(n) which is ~3162 iterations, well within the 1-second limit.

assumptions: Input n will be a standard integer.
The requirement for 10_000_000 in under 1 second is met by O(sqrt(n)) trial division with 6k optimization.
known_risks: For n very close to 10^7, the loop runs ~1666 iterations, which is extremely fast in Python, well within time limits.

assumptions: The input n is always an integer.
Performance requirement is < 1 second for n up to 10,000,000, which the 6k +/- 1 optimization satisfies easily.
known_risks: None identified. The algorithm is standard and efficient.

assumptions: Input n is an integer.
Performance requirement is < 1 second for n up to 10,000,000.
known_risks: None significant; this is a standard optimized trial division algorithm.

assumptions: The input n is an integer.
The maximum value of n is 10,000,000 as specified.
known_risks: None significant; this is a standard and efficient primality test.

assumptions: Input n is always an integer.
known_risks: None significant; the algorithm is standard O(sqrt(n)) and handles all edge cases.

assumptions: The input n is an integer.
The time limit of 1 second is measured on a standard modern CPU environment.
known_risks: For extremely large numbers (much larger than 10^7), a trial division approach becomes slow, but for the specified limit of 10,000,000, it is well within the 1-second threshold.

assumptions: The input n is an integer.
The time limit of 1 second is measured on a standard modern CPU environment for a single call or repeated calls totaling a significant workload.
The 6k +/- 1 optimization is sufficient for n up to 10^7 without needing a pre-computed sieve.
known_risks: For a single very large prime near 10^7, trial division is O(sqrt(n)), which is approximately 3,162 iterations. This is well within 1 second in Python.
If the task implied checking *all* numbers up to 10^7, a Sieve of Eratosthenes would be required instead, but the signature implies a single integer check.

assumptions: The input n is an integer.
The performance requirement for n up to 10,000,000 refers to the worst-case scenario (a large prime number).
known_risks: For extremely large numbers (much larger than 10^7), the O(sqrt(n)) complexity might become slow, but it is well within the 1-second limit for the specified range.

assumptions: The input n is an integer.
The time limit of 1 second is measured on a standard modern CPU environment.
known_risks: Trial division is O(sqrt(n)), which is efficient for 10^7 but slower than a pre-computed sieve if many calls are made. However, for a single function call, it is optimal for memory and speed.

assumptions: The input n will always be an integer.
The time limit of 1 second is measured for a single call to the function with n = 10,000,000.
known_risks: For extremely large numbers (much larger than 10^7), the O(sqrt(n)) complexity might become a bottleneck, but it is well within limits for 10^7.

assumptions: The input n is an integer.
The time limit of 1 second is measured for a single call to is_prime(10_000_000) or a reasonable number of calls, which the O(sqrt(n)) algorithm easily satisfies.
known_risks: Trial division is slower than a pre-computed sieve if the function is called millions of times, but for a single call or sporadic calls up to 10^7, it is significantly more memory-efficient and meets the 1s requirement.
